sentences.base_language: Today I have a free day and I want to explore the city.
sentences.target_language: اليوم عندي يوم إجازة وأريد أن أستكشف المدينة.
sentences.romanized: al-yawm 'indi yawm ijaza wa-urid an astakshif al-madina.
sentences.literal: The-today at-me day holiday and-I-want that I-explore the-city.
sentences.grammarNote: عِندي (indi) = I have, literally 'at me.' Arabic has no verb 'to have' — possession is expressed with عِند + pronoun suffix. أُرِيدُ أَن أَستَكشِف (urid an astakshif) = I want to explore — the pattern verb + أن + subjunctive is the standard way to express 'want to do' in Arabic.

sentences.base_language: I start the morning at a small cafe.
sentences.grammarNote: مَقهَى (maqhā) = café — from قَهوَة (qahwa, coffee). The مَفعَل pattern creates place-nouns: مَقهَى (place of coffee), مَطعَم (place of eating), مَكتَب (place of writing). The final ى makes it a defective noun that takes no case ending in indefinite form.

sentences.base_language: I order a coffee and sit by the window.
sentences.grammarNote: أَطلُب (atlub, I order/request) from root ط-ل-ب (t-l-b, to seek/request). بِجَانِب (bi-jānib, beside/next to) = a prepositional phrase: بـ + جانب (side). النَّافِذَة (an-nāfidha, the window) — sun letter ن causes assimilation. نَافِذ means 'penetrating/effective' — a window is literally 'that which lets through.'

sentences.base_language: The street outside is busy with people and cars.
sentences.target_language: الشارع في الخارج مزدحم بالناس والسيارات.
sentences.romanized: ash-shari' fi al-kharij muzdahim bin-nas was-sayyarat.
sentences.literal: The-street in the-outside crowded with-the-people and-the-cars.
sentences.grammarNote: مُزدَحِم (muzdaḥim, crowded) — Form VIII active participle from root ز-ح-م (z-ḥ-m, to crowd). بِالنَّاس (bin-nās, with the people) — the preposition بـ (with) merges with ال: بـ + ال → بال. الشَّارِع (ash-shāriʿ, street) — sun letter ش causes assimilation: ash-shāriʿ.

sentences.base_language: After coffee, I walk to a bookshop nearby.
sentences.target_language: بعد القهوة، أمشي إلى مكتبة قريبة.
sentences.romanized: ba'd al-qahwa, amshi ila maktaba qariba.
sentences.literal: After the-coffee, I-walk to bookshop near.
sentences.grammarNote: مكتبة (maktaba) means both library and bookshop in Arabic — context tells you which. Here it is a bookshop. Many Arabic words carry a wider range of meanings than their closest English translation, so context is essential.

sentences.base_language: I browse the shelves and find an interesting book about history.
sentences.target_language: أتصفح الرفوف وأجد كتاباً مثيراً للاهتمام عن التاريخ.
sentences.romanized: atasaffah ar-rufuf wa-ajid kitaban muthiran lil-ihtimam 'an at-tarikh.
sentences.literal: I-browse the-shelves and-I-find book interesting for-the-interest about the-history.
sentences.grammarNote: كِتَاباً مُثِيراً (kitāban muthīran) — both words carry tanwin accusative ـاً because كتاب is the indefinite direct object and مثير is its adjective. In Arabic, an indefinite noun and its adjective must both agree in case (here accusative) and both take tanwin. Root ك-ت-ب (k-t-b): كِتَاب (book), كِتَابَة (writing), كَاتِب (writer), مَكتَب (office).

sentences.base_language: I buy it and put it in my bag.
sentences.grammarNote: أشتريه (ashtarihi) = I buy it. The pronoun suffix ه (-hi, it/him) is attached directly to the verb. This is standard in Arabic: direct object pronouns are suffixes on the verb, not separate words. Compare: أشتري (I buy) + ه (it) = أشتريه.

sentences.base_language: In the afternoon, I walk to the public park.
sentences.target_language: في المساء، أمشي إلى الحديقة العامة.
sentences.romanized: fi al-masa', amshi ila al-hadiqa al-'amma.
sentences.literal: In the-afternoon, I-walk to the-park the-public.
sentences.grammarNote: الحَدِيقَة العَامَّة (al-ḥadīqa al-ʿāmma, the public park) — definite noun + definite adjective. Both carry الـ: this is the rule for attributive adjectives in Arabic: definite noun requires a definite adjective. Compare: حَدِيقَة عَامَّة (a public park — both indefinite). الحَدِيقَة from root ح-د-ق (to encircle/observe) — an enclosed garden.

sentences.base_language: Children are playing and families are sitting on the grass.
sentences.target_language: الأطفال يلعبون والعائلات تجلس على العشب.
sentences.literal: The-children play(they) and-the-families sit(they) on the-grass.
sentences.grammarNote: يلعبون (yal'abun) = they play. This is the masculine plural present form. Notice that when a verb comes after its plural noun subject, Arabic sometimes uses the singular verb form instead — either is grammatically correct, but the plural after the subject is more emphatic.

sentences.base_language: I sit on a bench and read my new book.
sentences.target_language: أجلس على مقعد وأقرأ كتابي الجديد.
sentences.literal: I-sit on bench and-I-read my-book the-new.
sentences.grammarNote: كِتَابِي الجَدِيد (kitābī al-jadīd) = my new book. When a noun has a possessive suffix (كِتَابِي = my book), it is treated as definite — so the adjective الجَدِيد also takes الـ. This is the rule: possessed noun (with suffix) = definite → adjective must carry الـ too.

إنه (innahu) = indeed it is / truly it is. إن (inna) is an emphatic particle that strengthens a statement — it signals confidence or feeling. Compare the plain هو يوم رائع (it is a wonderful day) with إنه يوم رائع (what a wonderful day / it really is a wonderful day).
